question: How do students join the Office Hours or live workshop sessions if the Zoom link isn’t public?

Students join through **YouTube Live**, not the public Zoom link. The Zoom link is only for instructors/presenters/TAs.

They should:
- watch the session on the **DataTalksClub YouTube channel**
- find the video URL in the **announcements channel on Telegram and Slack** before it starts
- submit questions in **Slido** (the link is pinned in the chat when live)

You can also watch live on the DataTalksClub [YouTube Channel](https://www.youtube.com/c/DataTalksClub).

Don’t post questions in chat as they may be missed if the room is very active.

question: I can’t find my name on the leaderboard — how do I locate my own entry?
answer_llm:
Click the **“Jump to your record on the leaderboard”** link — that will take you directly to your entry.

If you want to see or change the name shown publicly, go to **“Edit Course Profile.”**
answer_orig:
When you set up your account, you are automatically assigned a random name, such as “Lucid Elbakyan.” Click on the "Jump to your record on the leaderboard" link to find your entry.

If you want to see what your Display name is, click on the "Edit Course Profile" button.

<{IMAGE:image_1}>

- **First field:** This is your nickname/displayed name. You can change it if you want to be known by your Slack username, GitHub username, or any other nickname of your choice. This is useful if you want to remain anonymous.
- **Second field:** Change this to your official name as in your identification documents—passport, national ID card, driver's license, etc. This is mandatory if you do not want "Lucid Elbakyan" on your certificate. This name will appear on your Certificate!

question: Why does the leaderboard show a weird random name for me, and can I change it?
answer_llm:
That random name is your default public display name on the leaderboard. It’s assigned automatically when you create your account.

Yes, you can change it:

- Click **“Edit Course Profile”**
- Change the **first field** to the nickname you want shown publicly on the leaderboard
- The **second field** is your official name for the certificate

So the leaderboard name is separate from the certificate name.

question: Can I take this course at my own pace and still get a certificate, or is the certificate only for the live cohort?
answer_llm: You can take the course self-paced, but you **won’t get a certificate** that way. Certificates are only awarded if you finish with a **live cohort**, because you need to complete the peer-review part of the course while it’s running.
answer_orig:
No, you can only get a certificate if you finish the course with a "live" cohort.

We don't award certificates for the self-paced mode. The reason is you need to peer-review 3 capstone(s) after submitting your project.

You can only peer-review projects at the time the course is running; after the form is closed and the peer-review list is compiled.

question: If I complete all the lessons in self-paced mode, will I still be eligible for a course certificate?

question: Why does the homework content keep changing before it's released?
answer_llm:
If the homework title contains `[DRAFT]`, it means the homework is not ready yet.

The homework is only ready when both are true:

- the homework form is open on the course management platform, and
- the homework title does not contain `[DRAFT]`.

Until then, the content can still change. Working on it in advance is at your own risk because the final version may be different.

question: Why am I getting an OpenAI API error about insufficient credits when I run `responses.create`?
answer_llm:
You’re getting that error because your OpenAI API account doesn’t have enough credits or quota left. For `responses.create`, an **“Insufficient credits”** error means your billing setup needs funding.

To fix it:
1. Add credits to your OpenAI account via pay-as-you-go billing.
2. Make sure you’re using a model that’s available to your account.
3. If needed, create a new API key after adding credits and update it locally.

If you want, I can also help you distinguish this from a 429 rate limit error.
answer_orig:
You may receive the following error when running the OpenAI `responses.create` command due to insufficient credits in your OpenAI account:

```
OpenAI API Error: Insufficient credits
```

question: What does it mean if `responses.create` fails with "OpenAI API Error: Insufficient credits"?
answer_llm: It means your OpenAI account does not have enough credit/quota to run the request. You need to add credits or otherwise restore billing/quota in your account.
answer_orig:
You may receive the following error when running the OpenAI `responses.create` command due to insufficient credits in your OpenAI account:

```

```

question: How can I fix the error that appears when using OpenAI `responses.create` and says I don't have enough credits?

question: Why am I getting a 429 RateLimitError from OpenAI even though my code looks fine?
answer_llm:
Because `429 RateLimitError` can mean **either** a real rate limit **or** an **account quota/billing problem**.

In the course context, the most common cause is:

- **Your OpenAI API account has no available quota/credits**
- The error message often says **`insufficient_quota`**
- OpenAI API is **not free**, so you need to **add billing credits** and sometimes **recreate your API key**

What to do:
1. Check the exact error text. If it says **“You exceeded your current quota”** or **`insufficient_quota`**, it’s billing, not your code.
2. Add credits to your OpenAI account.
3. Make sure you’re using a model available to your account.
4. If needed, create a new API key after adding billing.

If you are seeing 429s during a notebook loop, that can also be a genuine rate-limit issue from too many requests per minute. In that case:
- reduce concurrency
- add retries with backoff
- cache results
- batch requests

So if your code “looks fine,” the likely reason is **account quota/credits, not a bug in your code**.

question: Why does the course use uv instead of pip and virtualenv for Python setup?
answer_llm:
The course uses `uv` because it’s fast and convenient. It replaces several traditional Python tools—like `pip`, `virtualenv`, `pip-tools`, `pipx`, `poetry`, `pyenv`, and `twine`—so you can manage Python versions, virtual environments, and dependencies with one tool and reproducible lockfiles.

You don’t have to use it; `pip` and `virtualenv` still work fine. `uv` is just simpler and faster, which is why the lessons use it.
answer_orig:
The course uses uv because it's fast and convenient. It's an all-in-one tool that replaces pip, virtualenv, pip-tools, pipx, poetry, pyenv, and twine — instead of juggling several tools, you manage Python versions, virtual environments, and dependencies through one fast command with reproducible lockfiles.

You're not required to use it — pip and virtualenv still work fine — but uv makes the setup simpler and faster, which is why the lessons use it.

Official docs: https://docs.astral.sh/uv/

question: What makes uv better or faster than the usual Python tools like pip, virtualenv, and poetry?
answer_llm:
`uv` is better mainly because it’s **fast** and **all-in-one**.

From the course context, it replaces several common Python tools at once:

- `pip`
- `virtualenv`
- `pip-tools`
- `pipx`
- `poetry`
- `pyenv`
- `twine`

So instead of juggling multiple commands and tools, you can manage:

- Python versions
- virtual environments
- dependencies
- reproducible lockfiles

with one fast tool.

It’s not required, though — `pip` and `virtualenv` still work fine. The course uses `uv` because it makes setup **simpler and faster**.

question: Why does my Jupyter notebook say OPENAI_API_KEY is missing even though I put it in .env?
answer_llm:
Make sure you:

1. Installed `python-dotenv`:
```bash
pip install python-dotenv
```

2. Loaded the `.env` file in your notebook **before** creating the client:
```python
from dotenv import load_dotenv
load_dotenv()
```

3. Checked that the variable name in `.env` is exactly:
```bash
OPENAI_API_KEY=...
```

If you still see the error, it likely means the notebook hasn’t loaded the `.env` file yet.

question: Do I need python-dotenv to read API keys in the notebook, and how do I load the env file?
answer_llm:
Yes — in this course, `python-dotenv` is the recommended way to read API keys from a `.env` file in a notebook.

How to do it:

1. Install it if needed:
```bash
pip install python-dotenv
```

2. In your notebook, load the `.env` file before using the keys:
```python
from dotenv import load_dotenv
load_dotenv()
```

3. Then read the values with `os.getenv`:
```python
import os

openai_api_key = os.getenv("OPENAI_API_KEY")
groq_api_key = os.getenv("GROQ_API_KEY")
```

Make sure your `.env` file contains the exact variable names, for example:
```bash
OPENAI_API_KEY=sk-...
```

Also, add `.env` to `.gitignore` so you never commit your keys.

question: Where exactly should I call load_dotenv() before making the OpenAI client in Jupyter?

Call `load_dotenv()` **right after your imports and before you create the OpenAI client**.

Example:

```python
import os
from dotenv import load_dotenv
from openai import OpenAI

load_dotenv()

client = OpenAI(api_key=os.getenv("OPENAI_API_KEY"))
```

So in Jupyter, put it in the **same cell** before `OpenAI(...)`, or in an earlier cell that you run first.

question: How do I keep my API keys in a .env file and load them in Python without hardcoding them?
answer_llm:
Store them in a `.env` file and load them with `python-dotenv`, instead of hardcoding them.

### 1) Create a `.env` file
```bash
OPENAI_API_KEY=sk-...
GROQ_API_KEY=gsk_...
GEMINI_API_KEY=...
```

### 2) Add `.env` to `.gitignore`
```gitignore
.env
```

### 3) Install `python-dotenv`
```bash
pip install python-dotenv
```

### 4) Load the variables in Python
```python
import os
from dotenv import load_dotenv

load_dotenv()

openai_api_key = os.getenv("OPENAI_API_KEY")
groq_api_key = os.getenv("GROQ_API_KEY")
```

This keeps your keys out of your code and out of Git.

question: What do I need to set up before I can call Gemini from the Module 1 notebook with the OpenAI-style endpoint?
answer_llm:
You need three things before calling Gemini from the Module 1 notebook with the OpenAI-style endpoint:

1. A Gemini API key in your `.env` file, e.g. `GEMINI_API_KEY`
2. An OpenAI client configured with Google’s OpenAI-compatible base URL:
   `https://generativelanguage.googleapis.com/v1beta/openai/`
3. The Gemini model name in your request, e.g. `gemini-3.1-flash-lite`

If you want, I can also show you the exact notebook setup code.
answer_orig:
To get started you need three things:

1. A Gemini API key saved in your `.env` file, for example as `GEMINI_API_KEY`.
2. An OpenAI client pointed at Google’s OpenAI-compatible base URL.
3. Your selected Google Gemini model name in your request.

Example code (loads the API key from `.env`, creates the Gemini client, and defines the `llm` helper):

```python
import os
from dotenv import load_dotenv
from openai import OpenAI

load_dotenv()

client = OpenAI(
    api_key=os.getenv("GEMINI_API_KEY"),
    base_url="https://generativelanguage.googleapis.com/v1beta/openai/"
)

def llm(instructions, user_prompt, model="gemini-3.1-flash-lite"):
    message_history = [
        {"role": "developer", "content": instructions},
        {"role": "user", "content": user_prompt}
    ]

    response = client.chat.completions.create(
        model=model,
        messages=message_history
    )

    return response.choices[0].message.content
```

This uses the older chat completions style via the OpenAI-compatible endpoint, whereas many course examples use the newer Responses format. That means you will need to change the notebook code in a few places, especially where it reads the model response and where it handles tools or function calls.

question: Why does the course code need changes when I use the chat completions API with Gemini instead of the Responses format?
answer_llm:
Because the course notebook examples often use the newer **Responses API**, but **Gemini via the OpenAI-compatible endpoint is used through the older chat-completions interface**.

That means a few things in the code must change:

- Read the answer from  
  `response.choices[0].message.content`  
  instead of  
  `response.output`
- If you use tools/function calls, the **tools schema may need adapting** to Gemini’s expected shape
- If you want structured output, use `chat.completions.parse` rather than `responses.parse`

So the code changes are needed because **the response format and API behavior are different** between OpenAI Responses and chat completions, and Gemini does not implement the OpenAI Responses API directly.

question: How do I get Pydantic-style structured output from Gemini through the OpenAI-compatible API if `responses.parse` isn’t an option?
answer_llm:
Use the OpenAI SDK’s **chat-completions parsing flow** instead of `responses.parse`.

1. Define a Pydantic model for the structure you want.
2. Call `client.chat.completions.parse(...)` with `response_format=YourModel`.
3. Read the parsed object from `response.choices[0].message.parsed`.

Example:

```python
from pydantic import BaseModel

class Question(BaseModel):
    question: str
    answer: str

class Questions(BaseModel):
    questions: list[Question]
```

```python
import os
from dotenv import load_dotenv
from openai import OpenAI

load_dotenv()

client = OpenAI(
    api_key=os.getenv("GEMINI_API_KEY"),
    base_url="https://generativelanguage.googleapis.com/v1beta/openai/"
)

messages = [
    {"role": "developer", "content": "Generate three FAQ questions about RAG."},
    {"role": "user", "content": "Topic: structured output with Gemini"}
]

response = client.chat.completions.parse(
    model="gemini-3.1-flash-lite",
    messages=messages,
    response_format=Questions
)

result = response.choices[0].message.parsed
print(result.questions)
```

This gives you parsed Pydantic-style output directly, with no manual JSON parsing.
answer_orig:
To get parsed structured output, use the OpenAI SDK's chat-completions parsing flow instead of the newer Responses API. This is the right choice when you want to stay on the OpenAI SDK but call a chat-completions-compatible model like Gemini through the OpenAI-compatible endpoint.

```python

    question: str
    answer: str
```

question: How do I install Ollama on macOS, Windows, or Linux?
answer_llm:
To install Ollama:

- **macOS**: Go to [https://ollama.com/download](https://ollama.com/download) and download the **`.pkg`** file, then install it.
- **Windows**: Go to [https://ollama.com/download](https://ollama.com/download) and download the **`.msi`** file, then install it.
- **Linux**: Run this command in your terminal:

```bash
curl -fsSL https://ollama.com/install.sh | sh
```

After installing, you can test it with:

```bash
ollama run llama3
```

- **Windows**: Download the `.msi` and install it.
- **Linux**: Run the following command in the terminal:

  ```bash
  curl -fsSL https://ollama.com/install.sh | sh
  ```

Once installed, open a terminal and type:

```bash
ollama run llama3
```

This command will:

- Download the LLaMA 3 model (~4GB).
- Start the model locally.
- Open a chat-like interface where you can type questions.

To test the Ollama local server, run the following command:

```bash
curl http://localhost:11434
```

You should receive a response similar to:

```json
{"models": [...]}  
```

Then, install the Python client with:

```bash
pip install ollama
```

```python
import ollama

    model='llama3',
    messages=[{"role": "user", "content": your_prompt}]
)

print(response['message']['content'])
```

question: Why does tiktoken show fewer tokens than the number OpenAI returns for the same chat request?
answer_llm:
`tiktoken` counts only the visible text you pass in, but OpenAI’s chat API adds some hidden formatting tokens around each message and at the start of the assistant response.

Specifically:

- each chat message adds about 4 structural tokens
- the API adds 2 more tokens globally for the assistant reply start
- extra whitespace/newlines or unusual Unicode can add a few more

So it’s normal for `tiktoken` to show fewer tokens than OpenAI reports for the same chat request.
answer_orig:
When using `tiktoken.encode()` to count tokens in your prompt, you might see a difference compared to OpenAI’s API response. For instance, you might get 320 tokens, while OpenAI reports 327. This is due to internal tokens added by OpenAI’s chat formatting.

Here’s what happens:

- Each message in a `chat.completions.create()` call (e.g., `{role: "user", content: "..."}`) adds 4 structural tokens (role, content, separators).
- The API adds 2 tokens globally to mark the start of assistant response generation.
- Extra newlines, whitespace, or uncommon Unicode characters in your content may slightly increase the token count.

Thus, even if your visible text is 320 tokens, OpenAI may count 327 due to these internal additions.

question: How can I avoid re-exporting my API key in every new terminal for this RAG project?
answer_llm:
Use **dirdotenv** so your API key loads automatically when you enter the project directory.

Quick setup:

```bash
uv tool install dirdotenv
```

Add this to your `~/.bashrc` or `~/.zshrc`:

```bash
eval "$(dirdotenv hook bash)"   # or zsh
```

Then, in your project folder:

```bash
echo 'OPENAI_API_KEY=sk-...' > .env
echo '.env' >> .gitignore
```

After that, you won’t need to re-export the key in every new terminal — it will load automatically when you `cd` into the project.

If you already use it, `direnv` is also fine.
answer_orig:
Use [`dirdotenv`](https://github.com/alexeygrigorev/dirdotenv). It is like `direnv`, but works with both `.env` and `.envrc`, and is more portable across shells and operating systems.

```bash
uv tool install dirdotenv

# add this line to your ~/.bashrc or ~/.zshrc:
eval "$(dirdotenv hook bash)"   # or zsh

# inside your project:
echo 'OPENAI_API_KEY=sk-...' > .env
echo '.env' >> .gitignore
```

After that, the key is loaded automatically when you `cd` into the project directory.

Important: always add `.env` and `.envrc` to `.gitignore` so keys never land on GitHub.

`direnv` is also fine if you already use it. In that case, create `.envrc`, add your exports there, and run `direnv allow`.

For GitHub Codespaces, use the built-in [Codespaces secrets](https://docs.github.com/en/codespaces/managing-your-codespaces/managing-your-account-specific-secrets-for-github-codespaces) instead of files in the repo.

For Python scripts, the equivalent is `python-dotenv`:

```python
from dotenv import load_dotenv
load_dotenv()  # loads .env from project root
```

question: How do I decide on field weights in minsearch or a similar search engine without just guessing?
answer_llm:
The systematic way is to **evaluate different field weight settings against a ground-truth dataset**, rather than guessing.

A practical approach:
1. Create a small set of representative questions.
2. Mark which documents should be retrieved for each question.
3. Try different field weights.
4. Compare retrieval metrics like **hit rate, precision@k, recall@k, or MRR**.

For small projects, trial and error can work, but **evaluation is the more reliable method**.

- duplicate text
- boilerplate
- OCR errors
- repeated headers
- repeated footers

question: Why does uv show a “Failed to hardlink files” warning in GitHub Codespaces, and do I need to worry about it?
answer_llm:
It happens because `uv` can’t hardlink files between the cache and the Codespaces environment, which is a limitation of that setup. It’s not an error.

Your package still installs normally, because `uv` falls back to copying the files instead.

If you want to suppress the warning in the current shell:

```bash
export UV_LINK_MODE=copy
```

To make that permanent:

```bash
echo 'export UV_LINK_MODE=copy' >> ~/.bashrc
source ~/.bashrc
```

So: no, you usually don’t need to worry about it.
answer_orig:

The package still installs. `uv` falls back to copying files.

```bash

```

To make it persistent:

```bash
echo 'export UV_LINK_MODE=copy' >> ~/.bashrc
source ~/.bashrc
```

See the `uv` documentation for more details: [https://docs.astral.sh/uv/](https://docs.astral.sh/uv/).

question: If I just work in Codespaces and don’t commit, does my code stay there somehow or can it disappear later?
answer_llm:
Your code is saved inside the Codespaces Linux VM, so it doesn’t vanish immediately just because you didn’t commit.

But yes, it can disappear later: Codespaces can stop, disconnect, be rebuilt, or be deleted. If that happens, uncommitted work may be lost.

So the safe practice is to commit and push regularly to your GitHub repo.
answer_orig:
The code is saved inside the Codespaces Linux VM.

However, you should still commit your work regularly. Codespaces can stop, disconnect, or be deleted later, and committing makes sure your work is stored in your GitHub repository.

question: Why do people keep saying I should commit often in Codespaces instead of relying on the Linux VM?
answer_llm:
Because the Linux VM in a Codespace is not a safe long-term place to rely on for your work.

Your uncommitted code is stored in the Codespaces Linux VM, but Codespaces can stop, disconnect, be rebuilt, or be deleted later. If that happens, uncommitted changes can be lost. Committing often puts your work in your GitHub repository, where it’s preserved even if the Codespace disappears.

So the rule is:

- **Stop/start only:** uncommitted changes usually stay in the VM
- **Delete/recreate/rebuild:** uncommitted changes may be lost
- **Committed code:** safely saved in GitHub

question: Why does WSL2 say my model needs more system memory even though my PC has enough RAM?
answer_llm:
WSL2 is not using all the RAM on your PC — it’s using only the amount allocated to WSL2 itself. So even if your computer has enough memory, WSL2 may still report that the model needs more system memory than is available inside WSL2.

To fix it, increase WSL2’s memory limit by creating a `.wslconfig` file in:

`C:\Users\YourUsername\.wslconfig`

Example:

```ini
[wsl2]
memory=8GB
```

Then restart WSL:

```bash
wsl --shutdown
```

After that, you can run `free` inside WSL to verify the new memory limit.
answer_orig:
Your WSL2 is set to use Y.Y GiB, not all your computer memory. To allocate more RAM, follow these steps:

1. Create a `.wslconfig` file under your Windows user profile directory: `C:\Users\YourUsername\.wslconfig`.

2. Include the desired RAM allocation in the file:

   ```ini
   [wsl2]
   memory=8GB
   ```

3. Restart WSL using the command:

   ```bash
   wsl --shutdown
   ```

4. Run the `free` command in WSL to verify the changes.

For more details, read [this article](https://www.aleksandrhovhannisyan.com/blog/limiting-memory-usage-in-wsl-2/).

question: My GitHub Codespace is stuck on "Finishing up" and never connects — is there a quick fix or do I need to recreate it?
answer_llm:
Try the quick workarounds first:

1. Go to **github.com/codespaces**
2. **Stop** the codespace
3. **Start** it again

If it still hangs on **“Finishing up”**:
- Open it in the **browser** instead of desktop VS Code
- Or try another browser, like **Chrome, Edge, or Brave**

If none of that works, **yes — recreate the codespace** as a last resort.

Also, if it looks like your setup disappeared, that can happen after a rebuild or a brand-new codespace. Your repo in `/workspaces` should persist, but system-level installs may need to be reinstalled.
answer_orig:
These are usually GitHub Codespaces reliability issues rather than a problem with the course, so there's no single guaranteed fix — but the following workarounds resolve most cases.

**Codespace won't connect / stuck on "Finishing up":**

- Go to [github.com/codespaces](https://github.com/codespaces), stop the codespace, and start it again.
- If it still won't connect, open it in the **browser** instead of desktop VS Code, or try a different browser (Edge/Chrome/Brave).
- As a last resort, delete the codespace and create a new one.

**"My setup is all gone":**

- The repo in `/workspaces` persists across stop/start, but a **rebuild or a brand-new codespace** starts from a clean image, and system/global installs outside your project don't always survive. Reinstalling is quick with `uv` (`uv sync` / `uv add ...`).
- **Commit and push your work often** — uncommitted changes survive a stop/start but are lost if you delete or recreate the codespace.

If Codespaces keeps being flaky for you, consider running the course locally instead — see "[Can I run the course locally instead of Codespaces?](026_aa310de435_can-i-run-the-course-locally-instead-of-codespaces.md)".

question: Why did my setup seem to disappear after a rebuild or after making a new Codespace?
answer_llm:
A rebuild or a brand-new Codespace starts from a clean image, so any setup done outside your project may not survive. That’s why it can seem like it disappeared.

Your repo in `/workspaces` does persist across stop/start, but system/global installs do not always carry over. Reinstalling with `uv sync` or `uv add ...` is usually quick.

Also, if you didn’t commit your changes, they can be lost when you delete or recreate the Codespace.

question: What survives if I stop and start a Codespace, and what gets wiped out when I delete or recreate it?
answer_llm:
If you **stop and start** a Codespace:

- The repo in `/workspaces` persists.
- Your **uncommitted changes** also survive.

If you **delete or recreate** a Codespace:

- It starts from a **clean image**.
- **System/global installs** outside your project may be wiped or not survive.
- Any **uncommitted changes** are lost.

Best practice: **commit and push often** so your work is safe.

question: Can I use something other than OpenAI for the RAG homework, or is the course locked to OpenAI only?
answer_llm:
No — the course is **not locked to OpenAI**.

You can use **any LLM provider** that works for you, including:
- **Groq, OpenRouter, DeepSeek, Gemini, Z.ai, Mistral**
- **OpenAI-compatible endpoints**
- **Hugging Face hosted models**
- **Local models** via **Ollama, vLLM, LM Studio**, etc.

Usually, you only need to change the **`base_url`**, **API key**, and **model name** in the course code.
answer_orig:
If the provider used in the course isn't available or is blocked in your region (or you simply prefer another one), you can use any other LLM provider — the course isn't tied to OpenAI. Just switch to something else:

- Hosted, OpenAI-compatible providers — e.g. Groq, OpenRouter, DeepSeek, Gemini, Z.ai, Mistral. The course code uses the OpenAI client, so you usually only need to change the `base_url`, the API key, and the model name.
- Open models via Hugging Face (e.g. Qwen, Llama) if you prefer hosted open-source models.
- Serve a model locally with [Ollama](https://ollama.com/), [vLLM](https://github.com/vllm-project/vllm), LM Studio, or anything else — no external API call at all, so regional blocks don't apply and you don't need a paid key. Most of these also expose an OpenAI-compatible endpoint, so the course code works with only a `base_url` change.
- Rent a GPU machine and serve the model there (e.g. with vLLM) if your own machine can't run the model you want. This gives you a private OpenAI-compatible endpoint to point the course code at — just remember to stop/delete the instance when you're done so you don't keep paying for it.
- A VPN also works if you just need to reach a provider that's blocked at the network level.

Anything with an OpenAI-compatible endpoint (or a locally served model) will work — pick whatever is available and convenient for you.

For a curated list of options, see [Awesome LLMs](https://github.com/DataTalksClub/llm-zoomcamp/blob/main/awesome-llms.md) in the course repo, which collects OpenAI API alternatives and tools for running models locally.

question: How can I stop 429 errors from popping up when my notebook makes lots of OpenAI calls in this module?
answer_llm:
Reduce the number of API calls before worrying about the errors after the fact. In this module, the main fixes are:

- **Use retries with backoff:**  
  ```python
  from openai import OpenAI
  client = OpenAI(max_retries=5)
  ```
- **Lower concurrency:** keep thread/process pools small, around **2–3 workers**.
- **Cache results:** save embeddings/LLM outputs to disk and reuse them on reruns.
- **Batch inputs:** send multiple independent items in one request when possible.
- **Use a cheaper/free model/provider:** `gpt-4o-mini` or Groq’s free tier (`llama-3.3-70b-versatile`).

If the error is actually `insufficient_quota`, that’s a billing issue, not a rate limit.
answer_orig:
Free-tier and low-spend OpenAI accounts have per-minute and per-day request limits that are easy to blow through when a notebook loops over many documents. Reduce the number of API calls before you worry about handling the error after the fact.

- **Retry with backoff.** The `openai` Python client retries automatically with exponential backoff; raise the limit so transient 429s resolve themselves instead of crashing the run:

  ```python
  from openai import OpenAI

  client = OpenAI(max_retries=5)
  ```

- **Lower concurrency.** In a thread/process pool, keep the pool small (2-3 workers) so you stay under the per-minute cap. A larger pool finishes one batch fast and then fails on the next.
- **Cache results.** Write embeddings and LLM responses to disk (JSONL, pickle, or a vector DB) and reload on re-run. Re-executing a notebook should not re-call the API for inputs you already processed.
- **Batch where possible.** Group independent inputs into a single request (e.g. embed a list of texts in one call) instead of looping one-by-one.
- **Use a cheaper/free provider.** `gpt-4o-mini` is cheap enough for the whole course; Groq's free tier (`llama-3.3-70b-versatile`) has generous per-minute limits via the OpenAI-compatible endpoint.

If you already see `insufficient_quota`, that is a billing issue, not a rate limit — see [OpenAI: Error: RateLimitError: Error code: 429](openai-error-ratelimiterror-error-code-429).

question: What exactly counts as a tool or function in agentic RAG?
answer_llm:
In agentic RAG, a tool or function is anything the model can call to get external help, for example:

- searching documents
- querying a database
- calling an API
- running a calculation

The key point is that the model decides when to use it. In basic RAG, retrieval is usually a fixed step before answering.
answer_orig:
In the context of this course, tools and functions are closely related terms. Do not worry too much about the naming difference.

A tool/function is something the model can call when it needs external help, such as searching documents, querying a database, calling an API, or running a calculation.

The important idea is that in agentic RAG, the model can decide when to call a tool/function. In basic RAG, retrieval is usually a fixed step that happens before the model answers.

question: Why do I get `AttributeError: 'str' object has no attribute 'output'` when I call OpenAI Responses API on a model from another provider like Mistral or Groq?
answer_llm:
Because `response.output` is part of the **OpenAI Responses API**, and models from other providers like **Mistral, Groq, Gemini, Anthropic, etc.** usually don’t implement that API shape.

So if you call a non-OpenAI model through the Responses API, you may get a plain string or a different response object, and then `response.output` fails with:

`AttributeError: 'str' object has no attribute 'output'`

Use the **chat-completions** API instead for those providers:

```python
response = client.chat.completions.create(
    model="<provider-model>",
    messages=[{"role": "user", "content": prompt}],
    tools=tools_schema,
)

return response.choices[0].message.content
```

If you use tools, you may also need to **adapt the tools schema** to match what that provider expects.

So the short answer is: **`responses.create()` / `response.output` is OpenAI-specific; non-OpenAI providers often require `chat.completions.create()` and `response.choices[0].message.content`.**
answer_orig:
The new OpenAI Responses API (`client.responses.create(...)`, accessed via `response.output`) is OpenAI-specific. Other providers (Mistral, Groq, Gemini, etc.) don't implement it.

For non-OpenAI providers, use the chat-completions API and read `response.choices[0].message.content`:

```python
response = client.chat.completions.create(
    model="<provider-model>",
    messages=[{"role": "user", "content": prompt}],
    tools=tools_schema,  # may need adapting per provider
)
return response.choices[0].message.content
```

You'll also have to adapt the tools schema to whatever shape your provider expects.

It is okay to use the older `chat.completions` API for homework or projects if your provider supports that interface better than the Responses API.

question: Why do I get Error code 400 saying tools[0].function is missing when I use Azure OpenAI for the weather tool?
What should the get_weather_tool JSON look like so Azure OpenAI accepts it without the missing required parameter error?
Do I need to wrap my weather tool inside a function block for Azure OpenAI, and what fields should it include?
How can I fix the tool definition for get_weather so the city parameter is properly required?
Is there a specific JSON format for function calling in Azure OpenAI to avoid the tools[0].function invalid request error?
Why do I get a 404 with toyaikit when I try Groq or another non-OpenAI model, and what should I switch to instead?
answer_llm:
You get the 404 because `toyaikit`’s `OpenAIResponsesRunner` / `OpenAIClient` calls OpenAI’s **Responses API** (`responses.create`), and that endpoint is **OpenAI-specific**. Groq and many other non-OpenAI providers don’t implement it, so the request fails.

What to switch to instead:
- Use the **chat completions** classes:
  - `OpenAIChatCompletionsClient`
  - `OpenAIChatCompletionsRunner`

These use `chat.completions.create`, which Groq and other OpenAI-compatible providers support.

If you’re using Anthropic, switch to:
- `AnthropicClient`
- `AnthropicMessagesRunner`

If you want, I can show the exact code change for Groq or Anthropic.
answer_orig:
If you get a `404` (or similar) when using toyaikit with Groq or another non-OpenAI provider, it's because `OpenAIResponsesRunner` / `OpenAIClient` call OpenAI's Responses endpoint (`responses.create`), which only OpenAI implements. Switch to the **chat completions** classes, which use the standard `chat.completions.create` endpoint that Groq and other OpenAI-compatible providers support.

### OpenAI or Groq (chat completions)

```python
import os
from openai import OpenAI
from toyaikit.tools import Tools
from toyaikit.llm import OpenAIChatCompletionsClient
from toyaikit.chat.runners import OpenAIChatCompletionsRunner

tools = Tools()
tools.add_tools(my_tools_object)   # functions need type hints + an Args: docstring

# OpenAI:
llm_client = OpenAIChatCompletionsClient(model="gpt-4o-mini", client=OpenAI())

# Groq (same runner, just point the OpenAI client at Groq's base URL):
groq_client = OpenAI(
    api_key=os.getenv("GROQ_API_KEY"),
    base_url="https://api.groq.com/openai/v1",
)
llm_client = OpenAIChatCompletionsClient(model="llama-3.3-70b-versatile", client=groq_client)

runner = OpenAIChatCompletionsRunner(
    tools=tools,
    developer_prompt="You are a helpful assistant.",
    llm_client=llm_client,
)
result = runner.loop(prompt="What's the weather in Berlin?")
print(result.last_message)
```

### Anthropic

```python
from toyaikit.tools import Tools
from toyaikit.llm import AnthropicClient
from toyaikit.chat.runners import AnthropicMessagesRunner

tools = Tools()
tools.add_tools(my_tools_object)

# Reads ANTHROPIC_API_KEY from the environment automatically:
llm_client = AnthropicClient(model="claude-haiku-4-5")

runner = AnthropicMessagesRunner(
    tools=tools,
    developer_prompt="You are a helpful assistant.",
    llm_client=llm_client,
)
result = runner.loop(prompt="What's the weather in Berlin?")
print(result.last_message)
```

Notes:

- `runner.loop(prompt=...)` runs one turn programmatically and returns a result with `.last_message`, `.all_messages`, and `.tokens` — no chat interface needed. Use `runner.run()` only for the interactive Jupyter loop.
- Pass an explicit, current model id. For Anthropic, `claude-haiku-4-5` / `claude-sonnet-4-5` work; an outdated id can 404.
- For Groq you'll see a harmless `UnknownModelWarning: No pricing data...` — the call still succeeds, only cost calculation is skipped.

question: Does toyaikit support non-OpenAI providers at all, or is the issue that I’m using the wrong runner/client classes?

  - `OpenAIChatCompletionsRunner`

- `AnthropicClient`
- `AnthropicMessagesRunner`

question: Why do I get a 402 error from OpenRouter when I call responses.create, and is it because my key is out of quota?
answer_llm:
Yes — in this case a **402 from OpenRouter can mean your request was rejected before the model ran**, and it’s often because the key is **out of quota or too limited for the implied max output**.

For `responses.create()`, OpenRouter checks billing/limits against the **maximum possible output tokens**. If you don’t set `max_output_tokens`, it may assume a very large value, and a free/low-limit key can get a **402 APIStatusError** even though the issue is not necessarily that the key is completely invalid.

### Fix
Set a lower `max_output_tokens`, for example:

```python
response = client.responses.create(
    model=os.environ["OPENAI_MODEL"],
    input=message_history,
    max_output_tokens=1024,
)
```

### So is it because the key is out of quota?
**Possibly, yes** — but more specifically, OpenRouter may be rejecting the call because of **quota/limit checks on the requested max output**, not just because the key is empty. This is different from OpenAI’s usual **429 insufficient_quota** behavior.

If it still fails after setting a reasonable `max_output_tokens`, then check:
- your `OPENAI_BASE_URL` points to OpenRouter,
- your OpenRouter key has remaining credits,
- you’re using a pinned model like `openai/gpt-oss-120b:free`.
answer_orig:
OpenRouter can return APIStatusError with code 402 when responses.create() is called without a reasonable max_output_tokens limit. This happens because OpenRouter bills/limits checks against the maximum possible output (which can be very large, around 65536 tokens), so a free or low-limit key can be rejected before the model runs. This is different from a direct OpenAI endpoint (which typically returns 429 for insufficient quota).

Fix

Pass a lower limit in your responses.create() call:

```python
import os
from dotenv import load_dotenv
from openai import OpenAI

load_dotenv()

client = OpenAI()  # uses OPENAI_API_KEY and OPENAI_BASE_URL from .env

response = client.responses.create(
    model=os.environ["OPENAI_MODEL"],
    input=message_history,
    max_output_tokens=1024,
)
```

For Module 1 homework with rag_helper.py, add the same parameter in the ``llm()`` method.

```python
response = client.responses.create(
    model=os.environ["OPENAI_MODEL"],
    input=message_history,
    max_output_tokens=1024,
)
```

For Module 1 homework Q6 with ToyAIKit:

```python
from toyaikit.llm import OpenAIClient

llm_client = OpenAIClient(
    model=os.environ["OPENAI_MODEL"],
    extra_kwargs={"max_output_tokens": 1024},
)
```

1024 is enough for homework answers; you can raise it later if needed.

If it still fails

1) Confirm your `.env` points at OpenRouter:

```env
OPENAI_API_KEY=sk-or-v1-...
OPENAI_BASE_URL=https://openrouter.ai/api/v1
OPENAI_MODEL=openai/gpt-oss-120b:free
```

2) Check your OpenRouter key limit and remaining credits at https://openrouter.ai/settings/keys

3) Prefer a pinned model (for example `openai/gpt-oss-120b:free`) instead of `openrouter/free`, which can route to models with different limits.

Note: This behavior is different from OpenAI’s typical 429 handling for insufficient quota. If you still encounter issues after these steps, double-check the model and endpoint configuration to ensure the key is valid and has sufficient credits.

question: If I still get 402 after setting max_output_tokens, what else should I check in my .env or OpenRouter setup?
answer_llm:
If you still get a 402 after setting `max_output_tokens`, check these in your `.env` / OpenRouter setup:

- `OPENAI_BASE_URL` should be:
  ```env
  OPENAI_BASE_URL=https://openrouter.ai/api/v1
  ```
- `OPENAI_API_KEY` should be your OpenRouter key:
  ```env
  OPENAI_API_KEY=sk-or-v1-...
  ```
- `OPENAI_MODEL` should be a pinned model, for example:
  ```env
  OPENAI_MODEL=openai/gpt-oss-120b:free
  ```
  rather than `openrouter/free`

Also check your OpenRouter key limits and remaining credits at:
https://openrouter.ai/settings/keys

If it still fails after that, I don't know.
answer_orig:
OpenRouter can return APIStatusError with code 402 when responses.create() is called without a reasonable max_output_tokens limit. This happens because OpenRouter bills/limits checks against the maximum possible output (which can be very large, around 65536 tokens), so a free or low-limit key can be rejected before the model runs. This is different from a direct OpenAI endpoint (which typically returns 429 for insufficient quota).

Fix

Pass a lower limit in your responses.create() call:

```python
import os
from dotenv import load_dotenv
from openai import OpenAI

load_dotenv()

client = OpenAI()  # uses OPENAI_API_KEY and OPENAI_BASE_URL from .env

response = client.responses.create(
    model=os.environ["OPENAI_MODEL"],
    input=message_history,
    max_output_tokens=1024,
)
```

For Module 1 homework with rag_helper.py, add the same parameter in the ``llm()`` method.

```python
response = client.responses.create(
    model=os.environ["OPENAI_MODEL"],
    input=message_history,
    max_output_tokens=1024,
)
```

For Module 1 homework Q6 with ToyAIKit:

```python
from toyaikit.llm import OpenAIClient

llm_client = OpenAIClient(
    model=os.environ["OPENAI_MODEL"],
    extra_kwargs={"max_output_tokens": 1024},
)
```

1024 is enough for homework answers; you can raise it later if needed.

If it still fails

1) Confirm your `.env` points at OpenRouter:

```env
OPENAI_API_KEY=sk-or-v1-...
OPENAI_BASE_URL=https://openrouter.ai/api/v1
OPENAI_MODEL=openai/gpt-oss-120b:free
```

2) Check your OpenRouter key limit and remaining credits at https://openrouter.ai/settings/keys

3) Prefer a pinned model (for example `openai/gpt-oss-120b:free`) instead of `openrouter/free`, which can route to models with different limits.

Note: This behavior is different from OpenAI’s typical 429 handling for insufficient quota. If you still encounter issues after these steps, double-check the model and endpoint configuration to ensure the key is valid and has sufficient credits.

question: In the Module 1 homework, what does it mean to point RAG at the chunk index instead of the original docs?
answer_llm:
It means you should build and search the RAG index using the **chunked documents**, not the original full documents.

So if your original data is in `documents` and you split it into `chunks`, you should do:

```python
index.fit(chunks)
```

not:

```python
index.fit(documents)
```

That way, the retriever returns only the relevant chunks and puts just those chunks into the prompt, which reduces the amount of context sent to the model.

question: Why does the homework want the index built on chunks rather than on the full documents?
answer_llm:
Because the homework is trying to make the RAG system retrieve and send only the **relevant pieces** of text to the model.

If you build the index on the **full documents**, retrieval works at the document level, so the system may pull in much more text than needed. If you build it on **chunks**, the search can return the specific chunk that matches the question, which keeps the prompt smaller and more focused.

In short:

- **Full documents** = too much context
- **Chunks** = more precise retrieval, less context sent to the model

That’s why the homework says to fit the index on `chunks`, not `documents`.

question: If I'm using a different LLM provider for the Module 1 homework, how should I count tokens from the model output?
answer_llm:
Use the token count from the model’s **response object**.

- If your provider is OpenAI-compatible, look for fields like `response.usage.input_tokens` or `response.usage.prompt_tokens`.
- If you use a non-OpenAI provider, check that provider’s own usage fields and adapt your code.
- Don’t use `tiktoken` or `cl100k_base` as a generic tokenizer for other providers like Gemini, Mistral, Hugging Face, or Groq, because tokenization differs.
- If the provider doesn’t expose token usage, use its **native tokenizer** as an approximation.

If it’s a multiple-choice question, choose the closest option.

For example, OpenAI-compatible clients usually return usage information on the response, such as `response.usage.input_tokens` or `response.usage.prompt_tokens`, depending on the API style.

If you use a non-OpenAI provider, check the provider's response object for its usage fields and adapt the code. Do not use `tiktoken` or `cl100k_base` as a generic tokenizer for Gemini, Mistral, Hugging Face, Groq, or other providers because tokenization differs by model.

If your provider does not expose token usage, use that provider's native tokenizer as an approximation. For multiple-choice homework questions, choose the closest option.

question: Why is my homework repo link getting rejected with a 500 or other non-200 error, and what should I check first?
answer_llm:
The checker sends a GET request to your submitted URL and expects an HTTP **200** response. If you’re getting **500** or another non-200 error, the first thing to check is whether the repo is **publicly reachable**.

Start with these checks:

- Make sure the repository is **public**
- Submit the **plain repo URL**, not one ending in **`.git`**
  - Good: `https://github.com/you/repo`
  - Bad: `https://github.com/you/repo.git`
- Double-check for a **typo** in the URL
- Open the link in a **private/incognito window**:
  - If it loads there, it should work for the checker too

If you want, I can also help you sanity-check a specific repo URL.
answer_orig:
The submission checker fetches the URL you submit with a GET request and expects an HTTP `200` response. A non-200 status (404, 500, etc.) almost always means the link isn't publicly reachable as-is. Check the usual causes:

- **Private repo** — make the repository public so the checker can access it.
- **Trailing `.git`** — submit the plain repository URL (e.g. `https://github.com/you/repo`), not `https://github.com/you/repo.git`.
- **Typo in the URL** — paste the exact link.

A quick test: open the URL in a private/incognito browser window. If it loads for you there, it'll work for the checker too.

question: What exactly are embeddings in vector search, and why do we turn text or other non-numeric data into numbers?
answer_llm:
Embeddings are a way of converting text or other non-numeric data into numbers while preserving meaning and context.

Why do this?

- Computers work very well with numbers and math.
- If two pieces of text are similar in meaning, their embeddings should be close together in vector space.
- That lets us use mathematical similarity methods to find related items, even when the exact words are different.

In vector search, these numbers form vectors, and we compare them using things like dot product or cosine similarity to find semantically similar content.

So, in short: we turn text into numbers so machines can measure meaning and similarity mathematically.
answer_orig: Embeddings refer to the process of converting non-numerical data into numerical data while preserving meaning and context. question: Why did the lesson use `.dot(...)` for cosine similarity instead of explicitly normalizing the vectors?
answer_llm:
Because the lesson assumed the embeddings were already **normalized to unit length**. In that case, the **dot product is exactly equal to cosine similarity**, so there was no need to normalize explicitly.

In the homework, the embeddings may **not** be normalized by default, so you need to normalize them first if you want the dot product to represent cosine similarity.
answer_orig:
In the lesson, `.dot(...)` was used under the assumption that the embeddings returned by the model (e.g., `model.encode(...)` from OpenAI) are already normalized to have unit length. In that case, the dot product is mathematically equivalent to cosine similarity.

In the homework, however, we use classic embeddings like TF-IDF + SVD, which are not normalized by default. This means that the dot product does not represent cosine similarity unless we manually normalize the vectors.

question: For vector search, is it better to embed just the answer text, just the question, or both together?
answer_llm:
It depends — there’s no single best choice.

The course says to treat it as an experiment on your dataset:

- **Embed the answer text only** — works because answers carry the semantic content.
- **Embed the question only** — works because user queries may resemble the indexed questions.
- **Embed question + text together** — often the best, but it’s longer and slightly more expensive.

Best practice: try all three and choose the one with the best **hit rate / MRR** on your ground-truth set.
answer_orig:
There's no single right answer — it's an experiment to run on your dataset. The course shows three options:

- Embed the answer (`text`) only — works because the model captures semantic similarity between questions and their answers.
- Embed the question only — works because user queries look like the indexed questions.
- Embed `question + " " + text` — often the best, but produces longer input and slightly more cost.

Pick whichever gives the best hit rate / MRR on your ground-truth set. The course materials include a side-by-side comparison.

question: What does cosine similarity actually measure between two vectors?
answer_llm:
Cosine similarity measures how **aligned** two vectors are, i.e. the **cosine of the angle between them**. It focuses on their direction rather than their magnitude.

- **1** = same direction
- **0** = orthogonal / no similarity
- **-1** = opposite direction

When vectors are normalized to unit length, cosine similarity is just the **dot product**.
answer_orig: Cosine similarity is a measure used to calculate the similarity between two non-zero vectors, often used in text analysis to determine how similar two documents are based on their content. This metric computes the cosine of the angle between two vectors, which are typically word counts or TF-IDF values of the documents. The cosine similarity value ranges from -1 to 1, where 1 indicates that the vectors are identical, 0 indicates that the vectors are orthogonal (no similarity), and -1 represents completely opposite vectors.

question: How is cosine similarity used in text analysis to compare documents?
answer_llm:
Cosine similarity is used to compare documents by turning each document into a vector, such as word counts or TF-IDF values, and then measuring the angle between those vectors. Documents with vectors pointing in a similar direction are considered more similar in content.

Its value ranges from -1 to 1:
- 1 means the documents are very similar
- 0 means they are unrelated
- -1 means they are opposites

In practice, when the document vectors are normalized, cosine similarity can be computed with a dot product.

question: Why do we use the cosine of the angle instead of just comparing the vector values directly?
answer_llm:
Because cosine similarity measures **direction**, not raw size.

If you compare vector values directly, vectors with larger magnitudes can look “more similar” just because their numbers are bigger. Cosine similarity ignores that by looking at the **angle** between vectors, so it tells you whether two vectors point in the same direction.

That’s especially useful for text embeddings, where the overall length of a vector may depend on document length or other scaling factors, but the **pattern of values** is what matters for similarity.

When vectors are normalized to unit length, the cosine similarity is just the dot product.

This metric computes the cosine of the angle between two vectors, which are typically word counts or TF-IDF values of the documents. The cosine similarity value ranges from -1 to 1, where 1 indicates that the vectors are identical, 0 indicates that the vectors are orthogonal (no similarity), and -1 represents completely opposite vectors.

question: Why does cosine similarity become just a dot product when the embeddings are normalized?
answer_llm:
Because if both vectors are **normalized to unit length**, their magnitudes are 1, so the cosine similarity formula

\[
\cos(\theta) = \frac{a \cdot b}{\|a\|\|b\|}
\]

becomes

\[
\cos(\theta) = a \cdot b
\]

since \(\|a\| = \|b\| = 1\).

So with normalized embeddings, cosine similarity is just the dot product.
answer_orig: Cosine similarity measures how aligned two vectors are, regardless of their magnitude. When all vectors (including the query) are normalized to unit length, their magnitudes no longer matter. In this case, cosine similarity is equivalent to simply taking the dot product between the query and each document embedding. This allows us to compute similarities efficiently using matrix multiplication.

question: How can we use matrix multiplication to compute cosine similarity between a query and all document vectors?
answer_llm:
If the query and all document vectors are **normalized to unit length**, then cosine similarity is just the **dot product**.

So if:

- `D` is a matrix of all document embeddings with shape `(num_docs, dim)`
- `q` is the query vector with shape `(dim,)`

then you can compute all similarities at once with:

```python
scores = D @ q
```

or equivalently:

```python
scores = np.dot(D, q)
```

Each entry in `scores` is the cosine similarity between the query and one document vector.
answer_orig: Cosine similarity measures how aligned two vectors are, regardless of their magnitude. When all vectors (including the query) are normalized to unit length, their magnitudes no longer matter. In this case, cosine similarity is equivalent to simply taking the dot product between the query and each document embedding. This allows us to compute similarities efficiently using matrix multiplication.

question: What changes in cosine similarity if both the query and embeddings are already unit length?
answer_llm: If both the query and embeddings are already unit length, cosine similarity becomes the same as the dot product. So nothing changes mathematically except you no longer need to divide by the vector norms.

question: Why does normalization let us ignore vector magnitude in similarity search?
answer_llm:
Normalization lets us ignore vector magnitude because it makes every vector have unit length, so only the direction matters.

In cosine similarity, what we care about is the angle between vectors, not how long they are. Once vectors are normalized, the dot product becomes equivalent to cosine similarity, so magnitude no longer affects the result.

question: Can you explain why cosine similarity can be calculated efficiently with embeddings in a matrix and one query vector?
answer_llm:
Cosine similarity can be computed efficiently that way because, once embeddings and the query are normalized to unit length, cosine similarity is just the dot product.

So if you put all document embeddings into a matrix `X` and one query into a vector `q`, you can compute all similarities at once with:

```python
X.dot(q)
```

This is efficient because matrix multiplication is optimized and avoids looping over each embedding one by one.

question: For Module 2 vector search, do I actually need to create another GitHub repository, or is making a fresh codespace from the same repo enough?
answer_llm:
Just a new codespace is enough — you do **not** need a new GitHub repository.

Create the Module 2 codespace from your existing repo, and keep your work committed there since you may delete that codespace later.
answer_orig:
Just a new codespace. A codespace is an environment (see *Can I run the course locally instead of Codespaces?*); you create it from your existing repository, so you don't need a new GitHub repo.

Use a separate codespace for Module 2 because the vector-search dependencies are fairly heavy. Keeping them isolated means you can simply stop or delete that codespace when you're done, rather than leaving the extra weight in your Module 1 environment. Setup is quick.

Since you may delete this codespace later, commit your work to your repository (see *What happens to code saved in Codespaces if I do not commit it?*).

question: Why does download.py stay stuck at 0% when trying to get model.onnx from HuggingFace?
answer_llm:
`download.py` is usually stuck at 0% because the connection to HuggingFace’s CDN is slow or blocked.

The workaround is to download the file directly in your browser from:

https://huggingface.co/Xenova/all-MiniLM-L6-v2/resolve/main/onnx/model.onnx

and save it as:

`models/Xenova/all-MiniLM-L6-v2/model.onnx`

After that, `download.py` will detect the local file and skip downloading it again.

question: Why do the matrix dot-product version and the for-loop version of vector search return slightly different scores?
answer_llm:
They can differ slightly because of floating-point precision.

`X.dot(v_query)` uses optimized matrix multiplication code, while the for-loop adds the values sequentially. The math is the same, but the numbers get rounded in a different order, so the last few decimal places may differ.

So the scores are not really wrong—just compare them with a tolerance, for example:

```python
np.allclose(scores, scores_loop, atol=1e-5)
```
answer_orig:
In [Lesson 4 (Vector Search)](https://github.com/DataTalksClub/llm-zoomcamp/blob/main/02-vector-search/lessons/04-vector-search.md) we compute the scores two ways — `scores = X.dot(v_query)` and the equivalent for-loop — and they can come out slightly different. This is normal, it's just floating-point precision. If you compare them with `np.allclose(scores, scores_loop)` you might get `False`, even though both compute the same dot products. They just add the numbers up in a slightly different order, and floating-point addition isn't perfectly associative, so the results can differ in the last few decimal places.

Under the hood, `X.dot(v)` runs optimized BLAS code that sums in a different order than a sequential Python loop. The math is the same; only the rounding differs, so the values aren't really "wrong."

Just compare them with a small tolerance instead of expecting an exact match:

```python
np.allclose(scores, scores_loop, atol=1e-5)
```

question: If both methods are doing the same dot product, why does `np.allclose(scores, scores_loop)` sometimes say they’re not equal?

Because `np.allclose` is checking for near-equality, and the two methods can differ by tiny floating-point rounding errors.

Even though `X.dot(v_query)` and the for-loop compute the same dot products, they may sum the numbers in a different order. Floating-point addition is not perfectly associative, so the final values can differ in the last few decimal places.

```python
np.allclose(scores, scores_loop, atol=1e-5)
```

question: Why am I getting IndexError: list index out of range when I try to access the best chunk in my vector search code?
answer_llm:
The `IndexError: list index out of range` usually means your embeddings and chunks are not aligned: the number of vectors in your search matrix doesn’t match the number of document chunks.

Make sure you generate embeddings directly from the chunk list:

```python
contents = [chunk["content"] for chunk in chunks]
X = embedder.encode_batch(contents)
```

Then `X` should have the same number of rows as `len(chunks)`, so accessing the best chunk index will work correctly.

```python

```

The number of rows in `X` should be equal to `len(chunks)`.

question: Why should the number of vectors returned by encode_batch match the number of chunks in my list?
answer_llm:
Because each chunk should produce exactly one embedding vector. If `encode_batch` returns a different number of vectors than the number of chunks, then you won’t be able to match each vector back to its corresponding chunk, which can cause indexing errors.

The safe pattern is:

```python
contents = [chunk["content"] for chunk in chunks]
X = embedder.encode_batch(contents)
```

Then `len(X)` should equal `len(chunks)`.

question: For Module 2 homework Q2, my cosine similarity doesn't match any multiple-choice answer — could I be using the wrong embedding model?
answer_llm:
Yes — the most common issue is using the wrong embedding model.

For Module 2 homework Q2, you should **not** use `sentence-transformers` or the lesson models like `multi-qa-mpnet-base-dot-v1` / `all-mpnet-base-v2`. Instead, use:

```python
from embedder import Embedder
model = Embedder()
```

A few other things to check:

- Make sure you embed the page’s **`content`** field, not the filename or full record.
- Compare it to the **query vector from Q1**.
- The `Embedder` returns **normalized vectors**, so the **dot product equals cosine similarity**.

If your result is still slightly different, choose the **closest option** — small numerical differences are normal.
answer_orig:

```python
model = Embedder()
```

Both approaches produce the same vectors for the same model, but if you embed with a different model (for example the `multi-qa-mpnet-base-dot-v1` model from the lessons, or `all-mpnet-base-v2`) you'll get a cosine value that isn't among the options.

Also check that:

- You're embedding the page's `content` field (not the filename or the whole dict).
- You're comparing against the query vector from Q1.
- The vectors are normalized — the `Embedder` returns normalized vectors, so the dot product is the cosine similarity directly.

If your value is still slightly off after using the right model, pick the closest option — small numerical differences are expected.

question: How do I set up Gemini, OpenAI, and Tavily API keys so Kestra can use them in the orchestration module?
answer_llm:
Set the keys as environment variables **before starting Kestra**. Kestra reads secrets from variables prefixed with `SECRET_`, and the value must be **base64-encoded**.

Use this:

```bash
export GEMINI_API_KEY="your-gemini-api-key-here"                              # used by AI Copilot
export SECRET_GEMINI_API_KEY=$(echo -n $GEMINI_API_KEY | base64)             # used by the flows
export SECRET_OPENAI_API_KEY=$(echo -n "your-openai-api-key-here" | base64)  # required for flow 3
export SECRET_TAVILY_API_KEY=$(echo -n "your-tavily-api-key-here" | base64)  # required for web search (flows 3, 5, 6)
```

Then restart Kestra:

```bash
docker compose up -d
```

In a flow, reference the secret **without** the `SECRET_` prefix:

```yaml
{{ secret('GEMINI_API_KEY') }}
```

Never commit API keys to Git.


```bash

```

Inside a flow, reference a secret **without** the `SECRET_` prefix:

```yaml

```

See the [setup lesson](https://github.com/DataTalksClub/llm-zoomcamp/blob/main/03-orchestration/lessons/03-setup.md) for the full walkthrough. Never commit your keys to Git.

question: Why does Kestra AI Copilot keep saying it can only help with creating Kestra flows, and what should I check first?
answer_llm:
Because the AI Copilot is likely **not getting a valid Gemini API key**, so it falls back to the canned message: “I can only assist with creating Kestra flows.”

What to check first:

- Make sure you set the **plain** `GEMINI_API_KEY` environment variable, not `SECRET_GEMINI_API_KEY`
- Confirm it’s set in the same shell you use to start Kestra:
  ```bash
  echo $GEMINI_API_KEY
  ```
- Then restart Kestra:
  ```bash
  export GEMINI_API_KEY="your-gemini-api-key-here"
  docker compose up -d
  ```

If it still doesn’t work, the key may be missing, mistyped, or rate-limited.
answer_orig:
This message means the AI Copilot didn't get a valid Gemini API key, so it falls back to a canned refusal. In Kestra's Open Source edition the Copilot only supports Gemini, and it reads the **plain** `GEMINI_API_KEY` variable (not the base64-encoded `SECRET_GEMINI_API_KEY` that the flows use).

Make sure you exported the plain key before starting Kestra, then restart it:

```bash
export GEMINI_API_KEY="your-gemini-api-key-here"
docker compose up -d
```

If it still fails, the key is usually missing, mistyped, or rate-limited:

- Confirm the variable is actually set in the shell you ran `docker compose up` from (`echo $GEMINI_API_KEY`).
- Generate a fresh key in [Google AI Studio](https://aistudio.google.com/app/apikey).
- If you've been running the agent/multi-agent flows a lot, you may have hit the free-tier quota (`429 Resource Exhausted`) — wait a minute and retry.

Note that the Copilot needs the **plain** `GEMINI_API_KEY` while the flows need `SECRET_GEMINI_API_KEY` — export both.


question: Why does my Kestra flow say the Gemini key is invalid even though I already set SECRET_GEMINI_API_KEY?
answer_llm:
The most likely issue is that `SECRET_GEMINI_API_KEY` is being encoded incorrectly. If you included a trailing newline or space when base64-encoding it, the decoded secret is wrong, so authentication fails.

Use `echo -n` and quote the variable:

```bash
export SECRET_GEMINI_API_KEY=$(echo -n "$GEMINI_API_KEY" | base64)
```

Also make sure of these:

- Restart Kestra after exporting the variable, e.g. `docker compose up -d`
- Set the variable in the same shell session you use to start Kestra
- In flows, reference it as `{{ secret('GEMINI_API_KEY') }}` — without the `SECRET_` prefix

If you want, you can also put the values in a `.env` file and pass it with `--env-file`.
answer_orig:
The most common cause is a corrupted base64 value. If you encode the key with a trailing space or newline, the decoded secret is wrong and authentication fails.

Use `echo -n` (which omits the trailing newline) and quote the value:

```bash
export SECRET_GEMINI_API_KEY=$(echo -n "$GEMINI_API_KEY" | base64)
```

Other things to check:

- You must **restart** Kestra after exporting, so the container picks up the new variables: `docker compose up -d` (or `docker compose down && docker compose up -d`).
- Reference the secret in flows **without** the `SECRET_` prefix: `{{ secret('GEMINI_API_KEY') }}`.
- Export the variables in the same shell session you start Kestra from.

A cleaner alternative is to put the keys in a `.env` file and pass it explicitly:

```bash
docker compose --env-file ./.env -f ./docker-compose.yml up -d
```

question: Why can’t I just use a notebook for these AI workflows instead of an orchestrator like Kestra?
answer_llm:
Notebooks are great for learning and experimenting, but they’re not enough for production-style AI workflows.

You use an orchestrator like Kestra because real workflows need things a notebook doesn’t handle well, such as:

- scheduling
- retries
- monitoring
- secret management
- reliably chaining multiple tasks together

Kestra is also used in the course to show how AI fits into workflows in a production setting, not just in notebook cells. It supports AI Copilot, RAG, and AI agents, and works with major providers like OpenAI, Gemini, and Anthropic.

So: use notebooks for exploration, but use an orchestrator when you need reliable, repeatable workflows.
answer_orig:
Notebooks are great for learning and experimenting, but real AI workflows need more than a script that runs once: scheduling, retries, monitoring, secret management, and reliably chaining tasks together. That's what an orchestrator like Kestra provides.

In this module Kestra is also the vehicle for the AI techniques the course is teaching: AI Copilot to generate flows from natural language, RAG to ground responses in real data, and AI agents that decide which tools to call. The goal is to see how AI fits into production-style workflows, not just notebook cells.

Kestra's AI plugins also work with any major provider (OpenAI, Gemini, Anthropic, and more), so you can swap providers in a flow without changing anything else. See the [module intro](https://github.com/DataTalksClub/llm-zoomcamp/blob/main/03-orchestration/lessons/01-intro.md) for the full motivation.

question: Is there a way to build a Kestra flow without writing YAML by hand, like a form or something?
answer_llm:
Yes — Kestra has a **no-code form editor** that lets you build a flow from a form instead of writing YAML by hand.

There’s also the **AI Copilot**, which can generate a flow from a natural-language prompt, and you can refine it with follow-up instructions.
answer_orig:
Yes. You have three options that require no hand-written YAML or code:

- **The no-code form editor** builds a flow from a form instead of YAML. It is form-based (not drag-and-drop like n8n), so it suits people who don't want to edit YAML directly.
- **The AI Copilot** generates a flow from a natural-language description, and you can iterate with follow-up messages to add tasks or change the order. See the [AI Copilot lesson](https://github.com/DataTalksClub/llm-zoomcamp/blob/main/03-orchestration/lessons/04-ai-copilot.md).
- **External agent skills** (Claude Code, Codex, Kestra CTL) let you generate flows and push them into Kestra from your editor without touching the Kestra UI.

Unlike Airflow, where business logic and orchestration logic are intertwined in Python, Kestra keeps the two separate: your Python script stays a plain script, and the workflow YAML just describes how and when to run it. This is closer to a cron job than to a traditional Airflow DAG.

For simple point-to-point automations (e.g. "post a Slack message when a YouTube video goes live"), a tool like Zapier or n8n may be simpler and cheaper. Kestra's advantage is observability, retries, and handling more complex or technical workflows in one place.

Yes, you can keep tweaking it after it generates a flow: you can send follow-up messages to add tasks or change the order.

If you want, I can also help you phrase the follow-up prompt.

question: Why does ChatGPT sometimes invent Kestra YAML fields like bucket or name for GCS uploads, and what's the right way to check the actual properties?
answer_llm:
Because ChatGPT is not automatically grounded in the Kestra plugin schema for your exact running version, it can “guess” plausible YAML fields that look right but don’t actually exist.

For example, with **GCS Upload** it may invent `bucket` and `name`, while the real property is:

```yaml
to: "gs://my-bucket/path/to/file"
```

The right way to check the actual properties is to:

- **Use the official Kestra plugin docs** for your version: https://kestra.io/plugins/plugin-gcp
- **Validate the YAML in your Kestra environment** to confirm it parses and runs
- Prefer **Kestra’s built-in AI Copilot**, since it is grounded in the current plugin schema for your running version

So the rule is: don’t trust the model’s guessed field names blindly — verify them against the plugin docs/schema.
answer_orig:
A general AI (e.g., ChatGPT) isn't grounded in Kestra's plugin schemas for your running version, so it can surface plausible but invalid property names (such as `bucket`/`name`) instead of the real, version-specific property. For example:

**GCS Upload** — invented `bucket`/`name` props vs. the real `to` property:

```yaml
# Incorrect (invented props)
- id: upload_to_gcs
  type: "io.kestra.plugin.gcp.gcs.Upload"
  bucket: "my-bucket"
  name: "path/to/file"

# Correct
- id: upload_to_gcs
  type: "io.kestra.plugin.gcp.gcs.Upload"
  to: "gs://my-bucket/path/to/file"
```

**BigQuery LoadFromGcs** — split `projectId`/`dataset`/`table` vs. the real `destinationTable`:

```yaml
# Incorrect (pseudo-split properties)
- id: load_to_bq
  type: "io.kestra.plugin.gcp.bigquery.LoadFromGcs"
  projectId: "my-project"
  dataset: "my_dataset"
  table: "my_table"

# Correct
- id: load_to_bq
  type: "io.kestra.plugin.gcp.bigquery.LoadFromGcs"
  destinationTable: "my-project.my_dataset.my_table"
```

To avoid this:

- Cross-check generated YAML against the official plugin docs: https://kestra.io/plugins/plugin-gcp
- Use Kestra's built-in AI Copilot, which is grounded in the current plugin schema for your running version.
- Validate and test your YAML in your Kestra environment to ensure it parses and runs as expected.

Note that plugin properties can vary by plugin version; what's correct in one release may be invalid in another. If you share a snippet, we can help verify it against the docs.

question: In Kestra, why would a BigQuery load flow use destinationTable instead of separate projectId, dataset, and table fields?
answer_llm:
It would use `destinationTable` because that’s the actual Kestra plugin property for `io.kestra.plugin.gcp.bigquery.LoadFromGcs` in the course context. The field combines the full target table into one value, like:

```yaml
destinationTable: "my-project.my_dataset.my_table"
```

Instead of separate `projectId`, `dataset`, and `table` fields, which are not the correct schema for that plugin/version.

question: Why do we need to track token usage in Kestra workflows when using LLMs?
answer_llm:
We need to track token usage in Kestra workflows because tokens are what LLM providers bill for. Monitoring them helps with:

- **Cost control**: estimate and control spend, and catch cost spikes early.
- **Prompt optimization**: see which prompts/responses use too many tokens so you can shorten them.
- **Guardrails**: prevent long-running or looping workflows from accidentally racking up a big bill.

Kestra can capture usage from the model response, like `usage.total_tokens`, so you can log or monitor it per run.
answer_orig:
Token usage is tracked because it maps directly to what LLM providers charge you for, and Kestra workflows run LLM calls on a schedule or in loops where costs add up fast. The main reasons:

- **Cost control**: tokens are the unit providers bill by, so tracking usage lets you budget, forecast, and spot cost spikes before they get expensive.
- **Prompt optimization**: seeing how many tokens each prompt and response consumes helps you tighten prompts and trim output without losing quality.
- **Guardrails**: in long-running or looping flows, usage monitoring lets you set thresholds and alerts so a runaway task doesn't quietly rack up a large bill.

In practice, each AI task in a Kestra flow can capture usage from the provider's response (for example `usage.total_tokens`), and you can surface those numbers in logs, metrics, or dashboards to keep an eye on spend per run.

question: Why does Docker Desktop say "mounts denied" when I try to run Kestra with a /tmp folder mount?
answer_llm:
Docker Desktop says **“mounts denied”** because it’s **not allowed to access the host path** you’re trying to mount into the container. With Kestra, this is often `/tmp/kestra-wd`.

Fix it by sharing that path in Docker Desktop:

1. Open **Docker Desktop**
2. Click the **gear icon** for **Settings**
3. Go to **Resources > File Sharing**
4. Click **+**
5. Add `/tmp` (or `/tmp/kestra-wd`)
6. Click **Apply & restart**

Then run `docker compose up -d` again.
answer_orig:
This error means Docker Desktop isn't allowed to access the host path the container is trying to mount (commonly `/tmp/kestra-wd` when running Kestra). Add the path to Docker Desktop's file sharing list:

1. Open Docker Desktop.
2. Click the gear icon (Settings) in the top right.
3. Go to **Resources > File Sharing**.
4. Click the **+** (Add) button.
5. Enter `/tmp` (or the specific path like `/tmp/kestra-wd`) and press Enter.
6. Click **Apply & restart**.

Once Docker restarts, rerun `docker compose up -d` to start Kestra.

question: Why do I keep getting `JSONDecodeError: Expecting value` when I try to parse the LLM response with `json.loads()`?
answer_llm:
The LLM is probably returning **extra text or a markdown code fence** around the JSON, so `json.loads()` fails at the very first character with:

`JSONDecodeError: Expecting value: line 1 column 1 (char 0)`

To fix it, force the model to return **raw JSON only**:

```python
response = openai_client.chat.completions.create(
    model='gpt-4o-mini',
    messages=[{"role": "user", "content": prompt}],
    response_format={"type": "json_object"},
)
parsed = json.loads(response.choices[0].message.content)
```

Also make the prompt explicit, e.g.:

```text
Output a JSON object with a single key "questions" whose value is a list of 5 strings.
Do not include any extra text, explanation, or formatting.
```

If your provider supports it, use its JSON mode too, such as Gemini’s `response_mime_type="application/json"` or Groq’s `response_format`.

```python
response = openai_client.chat.completions.create(
```

Most providers have an equivalent (Gemini's `response_mime_type="application/json"`, Groq's `response_format`, etc.).

question: Why does my Jupyter notebook kernel crash when I try to embed the ground-truth set in the evaluation notebook?
answer_llm:
Because the notebook runs out of memory. On small-RAM machines like default Codespaces or low-end laptops, loading an embedding model together with the rest of the notebook state can exhaust memory and crash the kernel.

Workarounds:
- Use a smaller embedder, like `sentence-transformers/all-MiniLM-L6-v2`
- Run the embedding step in a separate Python script and load the saved vectors back into the notebook
- Use a machine with more RAM
- Process the ground-truth set in batches and free memory between batches
answer_orig:
Small-RAM machines (Codespaces default, low-end laptops) run out of memory when an embedding model is loaded alongside the rest of the notebook state.

Workarounds:

- Switch to a smaller embedder. `sentence-transformers/all-MiniLM-L6-v2` (384-dim) is a common drop-in. Note: switching models will change your hit-rate / MRR numbers, so re-run the eval after the switch.
- Move the embedding step into a separate Python script that you run from the terminal, then load the saved vectors back into the notebook.
- Use a Codespaces machine type with more RAM (Settings → "Machine type" on a Codespace), or run locally.
- Process the ground-truth set in batches and free memory between batches (`del`, `gc.collect()`).
